intro: 1993. IBM just posted an $8 billion loss — the largest in American corporate history at the time. Wall Street, the press, and your own board agree on the plan: break the company into pieces and sell them off. You've just been hired from outside to run IBM, and you're not sure you agree.

realOutcome.summary: IBM was left for dead and became one of the great corporate turnarounds. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: The consensus plan is to split IBM into independent 'Baby Blues' — separate companies for mainframes, PCs, printers, storage. Everyone assumes the sum of the parts is worth more than the whole.

choices.text: Keep IBM whole — the integration is the value.
choices.reveal: Gerstner cancelled the breakup, betting that customers wanted one company to integrate their whole technology stack. Keeping IBM unified became the foundation of the entire turnaround.
ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The pieces compete alone in commoditizing markets. The one thing IBM could offer that no one else could — end-to-end integration — is destroyed by the breakup itself.

choices.text: Sell off the mainframe business to raise cash.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You sell the profitable core that funds everything else to appease the quarter. The cash runs out and so does the company's spine.

decisions.scenario: The bleeding has to stop. Turning the company around will require cutting costs deeply — meaning laying off tens of thousands of people, on top of cuts already made.

choices.text: Make the deep cuts to stop the $8B bleed.
choices.reveal: Gerstner made painful but necessary layoffs to stabilize the company. Survival first — you can't transform a company that runs out of money.

choices.text: Protect jobs and cut costs slowly.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You spare people in the short term and the losses eat the company alive. A slower bleed is still a fatal one.

choices.text: Freeze everything and wait for revenue to return.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The revenue doesn't come back on its own. Waiting just spends the cash you needed to actually change the business.

decisions.scenario: IBM's identity is selling hardware boxes, but margins there are collapsing. You could reinvent the company around services and solutions — helping customers make all their technology work together.

choices.text: Build IBM Global Services as the growth engine.
choices.reveal: IBM pivoted from selling boxes to selling services and integration. IBM Global Services became the largest part of the company and the engine of its resurgence.

choices.text: Stay a hardware company and defend margins.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You cling to a commoditizing hardware business as prices fall forever. Dell and others win the box war; IBM never finds a second act.

choices.text: Chase consumer PCs for volume and buzz.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You fight a brutal, low-margin consumer PC war instead of building the high-margin services business your enterprise relationships make possible.

decisions.scenario: The internet is arriving. You can bet IBM's future on open standards and 'e-business,' or push your own proprietary systems and networks the way IBM always has.

choices.reveal: IBM bet early and hard on the internet, coining 'e-business' and backing open standards and Java. It positioned IBM as the company that could help any enterprise get online.

choices.text: Push proprietary networks and systems instead.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You fight the open internet with closed IBM standards. Customers route around you to the open web, and IBM looks like a relic instead of a guide.

choices.text: Dismiss the internet as an overhyped fad.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You wait out 'the internet thing.' By the time you admit it's real, the consultants and startups have taken the business you should have owned.

decisions.scenario: IBM's culture is rigid — a dress code, internal religious wars over OS/2 versus Windows, 'the way we've always done it.' You can enforce the old sacred cows or kill them to serve what customers actually want.

choices.text: Kill the sacred cows; sell what customers want.
choices.reveal: Gerstner deprioritized losing crusades like OS/2, relaxed the rigid culture, and reoriented IBM around customer needs rather than internal dogma. Ending the holy wars freed the company to grow.

choices.text: Keep fighting Microsoft on the desktop with OS/2.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You pour resources into a desktop war you've already lost. Pride in OS/2 costs you the focus you needed for services and the internet.

choices.text: Protect the legacy culture and dress code.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You preserve the rituals of the old IBM and the mindset that nearly killed it. Culture eats the turnaround for breakfast.

bands.line: You refused the breakup and rebuilt a giant. Legend episode: WON.
Stabilized, but never fully transformed. A survivor, not a comeback story.
Broken up and sold for parts. 💀

endings.ctaLine: IBM tripled by refusing to break itself up. Today's agents flagged a giant everyone wants dismantled →
